\title[Reading a Remote Teammate's Body]%
{Reading a Remote Teammate's Body: Heart Rate and Variability on the Avatar to Support Coordination}

\author{Anonymous Author(s)}



%%
%% By default, the full list of authors will be used in the page
%% headers. Often, this list is too long, and will overlap

%% of authors' names for this purpose.
\renewcommand{\shortauthors}{Anonymous Author(s)}

%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
Networked multiplayer games are among the most inhabited yet distributed interactive spaces: teammates act together while sitting far apart, so bodily signals that colocated players read at a glance never cross the network. We present a method for closing that gap: translating Science and Technology Studies commitments, that measurement constitutes rather than reports what it names, that classification carries consequences, and that any reading of a body is situated \cite{nelson_meeting_1996, bowker_sorting_2008, haraway_situated_1988}, into a wearable interface layer that renders heart rate (HR) and heart rate variability (HRV) as an animated halo around each player's avatar, compositionally encoding two separable channels (pulse and brightness for activation, ring coherence for variability) while withholding emotion words. We report two studies. Study 1 tested the icon's legibility online with a chance-referenced forced-choice design: 36 participants read HR at 66 percent and HRV at 58 percent accuracy from animation alone, well above the 25 percent expected by chance, with interpretations ordered as designed and workload on the low side of the scale. Study 2 implemented the acquisition, classification, and rendering architecture Study 1 left as a proposed design, and evaluated it during live cooperative play: 12 three-player teams (36 participants) completed an Overcooked-style task under a no-cue baseline, a numeric HR/HRV display, and the animated cue. Teams supported a struggling teammate 3.9 seconds faster under the Icon Cue than under No Cue, matching an explicit numeric display on speed and awareness while costing significantly less in perceived invasiveness and workload, though the numeric display was rated more useful for coordination and more trustworthy, a trade-off participants named directly. We contribute the STS-to-visual-encoding method itself, a compositional two-channel encoding and a chance-referenced legibility test as secondary novelties, and converging evidence that ambient bodily cues steady coordination across a distributed interactive space at a lower privacy cost than explicit disclosure.
\end{abstract}

\maketitle

\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}
Networked multiplayer games are among the largest settings where people cooperate with others they cannot see. Strangers and friends assemble into teams, divide roles, and steer shared outcomes through fast rounds of coordination, held together by communication whose quality tracks performance: communication volume and structure separate strong teams from weak ones \cite{bisberg_communication_2025}, and even a signal as thin as a map ping measurably shifts a competitive match \cite{leavitt_ping_2016}.

One signal that teammates in a shared room read without effort never survives the network. In person, people register a partner's quickened breath, restless posture, and rising tension continuously and prereflectively, folding that reading into how they speak and act \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}. Distributed play strips this bodily layer away: a remote partner must infer effort, composure, and withdrawal from text, voice, and avatar motion alone, and removing face to face cues measurably makes collaborators understand and judge one another worse than when a representation of the partner's bodily state is restored \cite{tan_role_2014}. A teammate who has gone quiet may be concentrating, overloaded, or withdrawing, and the three call for opposite responses; absent any read on the body, players guess, and wrong guesses accumulate into misattributed blame, mistimed help, and coordination that unravels under pressure. The problem this paper addresses is how to return a usable trace of that missing bodily layer to distributed teammates.

Sending physiological data across a network is not new: heart rate has been carried into chat to raise empathy \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017}, into shared displays to deepen social connection \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, moge_shared_2022}, and into cooperative and virtual games to strengthen felt bonds \cite{jarvela_augmented_2021, hirsch_my_2023, jiang_intimasea_2023, muller_intra-_2013}. Two limits recur: a long affective computing tradition treats a biosignal as evidence for an underlying emotion to be detected and named \cite{picard_affective_1997, mandryk_fuzzy_2007}, fixing a single interpretation onto a body whose signals are notoriously ambiguous, and studies reporting warmer feelings between partners seldom test whether a viewer can actually read the state on display. Legibility stays largely unmeasured.

We take a different route, shaped by Science and Technology Studies. If measurement helps constitute the phenomenon it appears merely to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, if the categories an interface imposes carry consequences of their own \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and if every reading of a body belongs to a particular viewer and moment \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, then an interface that stamps a teammate's heartbeat with the word calm or panic overreaches. Our display withholds such verdicts, rendering Apple Watch heart rate as pulse rate and brightness, and heart rate variability as the coherence of a ring, shown as a halo around each teammate's avatar (Figure~\ref{fig:teaser}): graded, deliberately open cues a teammate weighs against the unfolding match rather than reads off as fact \cite{suchman_plans_1987, wakkary_things_2021}. The artifact is built to make an argument, not a diagnosis.

We first evaluated whether that argument is even readable. In an online study, 36 participants viewed all sixteen combinations of four heart rate states and four variability states and reported what each conveyed. Participants recovered heart rate at 66 percent and variability at 58 percent from animation alone, well past the 25 percent expected from guessing, with interpretations ordered as intended and workload on the low side of the scale; several drew boundaries around exposing bodily data to teammates, a concern we take up directly. That study answers whether the icon is readable in isolation, not whether reading it changes what teammates do together, so we conducted a second study carrying the validated icon system into live cooperative play. Study 2 implements the acquisition, classification, and avatar-rendering architecture that Study 1 left unbuilt, embeds it in an Overcooked-style \cite{carroll_utility_2019, baek_toward_2022} cooperative cooking task, and evaluates it against a no-cue baseline and an explicit numeric HR/HRV display across scripted teammate-pressure events. Twelve three-player teams supported a struggling teammate 3.9 seconds faster under the Icon Cue than under No Cue, matching the Numeric Cue's speed while costing significantly less in perceived invasiveness and workload, though the Numeric Cue was rated somewhat more useful for coordination and more trustworthy. Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods} reports this second study in full.

We contribute: a design method translating Science and Technology Studies commitments into concrete visual encoding choices that offer social cues without assigning emotions; a peripheral avatar display compositionally encoding heart rate and variability as separable visual channels rather than transmitting heart rate alone, as almost all prior shared-physiology systems do; empirical evidence, from a chance-referenced forced-choice legibility study, that this label free representation is legible, graded, and light enough in workload to be usable, together with the privacy limits players place around it; an implemented, live-multiplayer instantiation of the architecture, evaluated under no-cue, numeric-cue, and icon-cue conditions against scripted teammate-pressure events; and evidence that the ambient cue speeds teammate support as much as an explicit numeric display while costing less workload and invasiveness, alongside a legibility-versus-precision trade-off that qualifies the case for ambiguity as a design resource.

\section{Literature Review}

\subsection{Communication and Coordination in Distributed Play}
Multiplayer games reward study less as game systems than as places where scattered groups organize themselves. Communication volume and structure separate skilled teams from weak ones across large player populations \cite{bisberg_communication_2025}, and a signal as spare as a positional ping alters who wins a competitive round \cite{leavitt_ping_2016}. The channels behind these findings are deliberate and symbolic: messages, calls, pings. The involuntary bodily signals that regulate teamwork in a shared room, the ones a partner registers before either speaks \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, reach the game through no channel at all. This section traces how prior work has tried to carry the body across a network, and where each attempt stops short of the reading a teammate needs.


\subsection{Reading Physiology as Emotion, and Sharing It Between People}
One tradition treats the body as a store of emotion to be recovered by computation. Affective computing set the agenda of sensing a signal and inferring the feeling behind it \cite{picard_affective_1997}, and play research applied it directly, mapping cardiac, muscular, and electrodermal activity onto models of a player's emotional state \cite{mandryk_fuzzy_2007, ravaja_phasic_2006} and adjusting game difficulty from those readings \cite{nacke_biofeedback_2011}, a lineage now broad enough to have its own systematic reviews \cite{navarro_biofeedback_2021}. Surveys record both the reach of this approach and its persistent snag \cite{gan_human-computer_2022, robinson_lets_2020}: one elevated heart rate accompanies fear, exertion, and delight alike, so a system that prints a single emotion word onto it throws away the ambiguity the body genuinely carries. That interpretive move is the one our design declines. A related strand of play research uses heart rate not to name a feeling but to drive mechanics directly, for instance rewarding elevated exertion with in game power ups \cite{ketcheson_designing_2015}; this treats the signal as a control input rather than a cue for a teammate to interpret, which is the reading problem our work addresses instead.

A second line sends the signal to another person rather than to a classifier: heart rate has been shared to build social connection \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, moge_shared_2022}, folded into mobile chat to lift empathy and awareness \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017}, and visualized during remote collaboration, where it raised group cohesion and perceived warmth over voice alone \cite{tan_role_2014}. In play, coupled and visualized biosignals have deepened the bond between partners in cooperative and virtual settings \cite{jarvela_augmented_2021, hirsch_my_2023, jiang_intimasea_2023, muller_intra-_2013}, consistent with evidence that physiological alignment tracks social closeness \cite{dikker_brain--brain_2017}. Shared cardiac signals have also entered more adversarial play, where visible biosignals become a strategic resource that players read to infer a hidden role in a social deception game \cite{fang_cardiac_2022}, which extends the sharing tradition beyond cooperation but leaves the same gap: readers act on the signal without a check that they identify it correctly. This work shows convincingly that a shared bodily signal changes how people relate. It leaves two things untested. It seldom asks whether a viewer can name the state on show, as distinct from feeling closer to its owner, and it seldom removes the emotional framing to check that the representation still communicates. Legibility, the precondition for acting on a cue mid match, is rarely what these studies measure.

\subsection{Situated Interpretation and a Science and Technology Studies Stance}
Where the first tradition locates meaning in the signal and the second in the bond, a third locates it in the moment of use. Situated and embodied accounts hold that action is read against an unfolding context rather than decoded from a fixed symbol \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, and design research treats an artifact as a way to pose that question rather than close it \cite{wakkary_things_2021}. Science and Technology Studies sharpens the stakes for anything that measures a body: instruments help produce the phenomena they seem only to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, classifications carry consequences for those they sort \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and no reading of a body is a view from nowhere \cite{haraway_situated_1988}. Together these commitments argue against an interface that resolves a teammate's heartbeat into a verdict, and for one that offers a graded, open cue to be interpreted in place.

\subsection{Positioning the Present Work}
The closest prior art is Howell et al.'s ambiguous social display of skin conductance \cite{howell_biosignals_2016}, which already withholds emotion labels and treats interpretive openness as a design resource, the same design move this paper makes. We differ from it, and from the wider literature, in three specific ways rather than in the ambiguity commitment itself. First, where shared physiology systems, Howell et al.'s included, almost always transmit a single signal, we add heart rate variability as a second, separately mapped visual dimension, because the two indices carry different information: rate reflects momentary activation, while variability reflects the autonomic regulation underneath it \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007}. Second, where ambiguous displays are typically evaluated through open-ended qualitative interpretation, we additionally quantify legibility with a forced-choice identification accuracy tested against a fixed chance level, which lets us report how much of the intended state a label free display still communicates rather than only whether it prompts interpretation. Third, where earlier displays are judged by the connection they produce, we judge ours by the reading it supports, testing whether a label free representation is legible before asking what it does socially. We do not claim ambiguity itself as new; we claim a compositional two-channel encoding and a chance-referenced legibility test as the specific extensions this paper adds to that line of work. Table~\ref{tab:related} sets the contrast out in full.

\begin{table*}[t]
\scriptsize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.92}
\caption{Systems that carry physiological data between people, and how the present work differs. \emph{Emotion labels} marks whether the display names a feeling (Named), shows a raw or connection oriented signal that implies one (Implicit), or names none (None). \emph{Reader legibility} marks whether the study tested that a viewer can identify the intended state from the display itself.}
\label{tab:related}
\begin{tabular}{@{}l p{2.1cm} p{2.8cm} p{1.1cm} p{1.5cm}@{}}
\toprule
Work & Signal and setting & Primary aim & Emotion labels & Reader legibility tested \\
\midrule
Picard \cite{picard_affective_1997} & Multiple signals, no shared display & Recognize and model affect by computation & Named & No \\
Mandryk and Atkins \cite{mandryk_fuzzy_2007} & Cardiac, muscular, electrodermal in play & Infer a player's emotion from physiology & Named & No \\
Slov\'ak et al. \cite{slovak_understanding_2012} & Heart rate, shared display & Support social connection & Implicit & No \\
Hassib et al. \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017} & Heart rate, mobile chat & Raise empathy and awareness & Implicit & No \\
Tan et al. \cite{tan_role_2014} & Physiological signals, remote collaboration & Restore awareness and cohesion at a distance & Implicit & Indirect \\
Moge et al. \cite{moge_shared_2022} & Heart rate, cooperative game & Enrich shared play & Implicit & No \\
Hirsch et al. \cite{hirsch_my_2023} & Heartbeat, avatar in a VR game & Increase social connectedness & Implicit & No \\
Fang et al. \cite{fang_cardiac_2022} & Cardiac signals, social deception game & Study biosignals as a strategic resource & Implicit & No \\
Howell et al. \cite{howell_biosignals_2016} & Skin conductance, ambiguous shared display & Explore ambiguity and emotional interpretation & None & Partial (qualitative, not forced-choice) \\
\textbf{This work} & Heart rate and variability, peripheral avatar cue & Make a teammate's state readable as a coordination cue & None & Yes (forced-choice against chance) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table*}

\section{Study 1: Icon Legibility Validation}
\label{sec:study1-methods}
\noindent
This paper reports two studies. Study 1, described in this section, used a research through design methodology with two phases: in the design phase, we derived the icon system from Science and Technology Studies and built the single-avatar browser stimuli that render it; in the evaluation phase, we tested the resulting visual language for legibility and interpretation in a controlled online study. Study 2 (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) then implements the same icon system as a live multiplayer prototype and evaluates it during cooperative play.

\subsection{Research Questions and Hypotheses}
\label{sec:study1-rqh}
We addressed three research questions.
\begin{description}
    \item[RQ1] Can players identify a teammate's heart rate state and heart rate variability state from the animated icon alone, without numbers or emotion words?
    \item[RQ2] Do the two visual channels carry the graded social meaning they were designed to carry, that is, does activation read as urgency and arousal, and does declining ring coherence read as lower stability and a stronger call for support?
    \item[RQ3] Is the display clear and light enough in workload to be usable during cooperative play?
\end{description}

We set five hypotheses.
\begin{description}
    \item[H1] Identification accuracy for both the heart rate state and the variability state exceeds the 25 percent chance level of a choice among four options.
    \item[H2] Perceived urgency and arousal rise monotonically with the heart rate state.
    \item[H3] Perceived stability falls as ring coherence declines across the variability states.
    \item[H4] The intention to offer support rises as ring coherence declines.
    \item[H5] The display registers low workload on the Task Load Index while returning high clarity and coordination usefulness.
\end{description}

\subsection{Design Rationale}
Rendering a teammate's cardiovascular state at all presumes that state is worth showing. Interoception, the continuous and largely prereflective sense of one's own physiological condition, is how a person normally accesses that information about themselves \cite{craig_how_2002}; the display's task is to give a second party partial access to a process the wearer otherwise senses alone. Three commitments from Science and Technology Studies fixed how we did that. Because measurement helps constitute the phenomenon it appears to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, we classified heart rate and variability relative to each player's own baseline rather than against an absolute clinical scale. Because the categories an interface installs carry consequences and harden into infrastructure \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, we used a small set of relative visual states and no named emotion. Because any reading of a body is situated and partial \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, and meaning forms in place rather than inside a fixed symbol \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, we left the cues open for interpretation against the surrounding play, following prior evidence that one bodily signal supports many readings depending on its context \cite{howell_biosignals_2016}. We pursued the design itself as inquiry through research creation \cite{amos_kyriaki_2015} and speculative design \cite{wakkary_things_2021}, treating the body as a resource for interface design in its own right rather than only a source of data to be extracted \cite{dauden_roquet_body_2020}.

\subsection{Icon System and Stimuli}
The stimulus set was the icon system itself. Heart rate, an index of momentary cardiovascular activation, controlled pulse speed, glow, and halo scale. Heart rate variability, a marker of autonomic regulation estimated from the interval between beats \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007, shaffer_overview_2017}, controlled the coherence of a surrounding ring, from a smooth continuous halo to a broken and jittering one. Each channel held four states defined relative to baseline, producing 16 combinations through the crossing of four heart rate states and four variability states. We rendered each state as a looping animation of five to seven seconds around a single avatar. Because we composed the two channels rather than assigning unrelated appearances, a participant who had learned each channel could read a combination not seen before. No state carried an emotion word.

Table~\ref{tab:hr-hrv-stimulus-classification} defines the four heart-rate states and four HRV states used as prototype design states rather than medical diagnoses, with HRV expressed as a percentage of baseline HRV using an RMSSD-style design logic.

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\caption{Heart-rate and heart-rate-variability stimulus classification used in the prototype. Thresholds were defined relative to each player's own baseline and were used to structure the simulated visual states rather than to provide medical, clinical, or emotional diagnoses.}
\label{tab:hr-hrv-stimulus-classification}
\scriptsize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.95}
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}p{0.17\textwidth}YYY@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{State} &
\textbf{Physiological meaning} &
\textbf{Prototype threshold} &
\textbf{Icon effect} \\
\midrule
\textbf{HR1: Resting / Low Activation} &
Calm or low cardiovascular activation &
Baseline to $+5$ bpm, or $\leq 10\%$ above baseline &
Slow halo pulse and soft glow \\
\textbf{HR2: Focused Activation} &
Engaged, alert, and mildly activated &
$+6$ to $+15$ bpm, or $11$ to $25\%$ above baseline &
Medium pulse and clearer glow \\
\textbf{HR3: High Activation} &
Intense engagement, pressure, or effort &
$+16$ to $+30$ bpm, or $26$ to $45\%$ above baseline &
Fast pulse and stronger glow \\
\textbf{HR4: Overdrive Activation} &
Very high activation and possible overload &
More than $+30$ bpm, or more than $45\%$ above baseline &
Rapid pulse, sharp glow, and stronger expansion \\
\midrule
\textbf{V1: High Coherence} &
High variability and flexible regulation &
$\geq 110\%$ of baseline HRV &
Smooth continuous ring, wide breathing halo, and stable motion \\
\textbf{V2: Stable Variability} &
Normal or baseline variability &
$90$ to $109\%$ of baseline HRV &
Clean dotted ring and regular halo motion \\
\textbf{V3: Reduced Variability} &
Lower flexibility and possible physiological strain &
$60$ to $89\%$ of baseline HRV &
Denser segmented ring with a slight wobble \\
\textbf{V4: Suppressed / Fragmented Variability} &
Very low variability and reduced regulatory stability &
Less than $60\%$ of baseline HRV &
Broken ring, visible jitter, and irregular opacity \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\end{table*}

Table~\ref{tab:hr-hrv-stimulus-classification} summarizes the baseline-relative HR and HRV classifications used to generate the sixteen animated stimulus conditions. We label the four heart rate states HR1 to HR4 and the four variability states V1 to V4 throughout; these condition labels are distinct from the H1 to H5 hypotheses stated in Section~\ref{sec:study1-rqh}.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.82\textwidth]{Icon.jpg}
  \caption{The icon design evolves through theoretical perspectives from Science and Technology Studies (STS), transforming an abstract sensing field into a human avatar surrounded by a dynamic halo. The final icon visualizes physiological signals as an ambiguous social cue, where halo brightness, pulse, density, and stability vary with heart rate (HR) and heart rate variability (HRV).}
  \Description{A visual design figure showing variations in avatar shapes and layered halo structures used to represent changes in heart rate and heart-rate variability.}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Study Design}
We used a within participants design in which every participant viewed all 16 states, removing between person variation from the central comparisons, matching recent within participants recognition tests of icon sets in HCI \cite{schafer_shape_2023} and recent work evaluating heart-rate-driven displays through dedicated player facing studies \cite{tu_biofeedback_2025}. We administered it online in two phases: an open phase presenting 4 states per participant with all labels hidden, and a structured phase presenting all 16 states in randomised order, for 20 rating trials per participant (144 open-phase and 576 structured-phase trials across the sample), followed by one closing questionnaire per participant. The design deliberately tests absolute legibility against a fixed chance level rather than comparative legibility against an alternative encoding, following the convention of ISO 9186 symbol-comprehension testing \cite{iso_9186_2014}. We did not include a numeric or emotion-labeled comparison arm; Section~\ref{sec:limitations} returns to what that omission does and does not let us claim, and Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods} reports a follow-up study designed to make exactly that comparison.

\subsection{Participants, Apparatus, and Procedure}
We recruited thirty six adults through the author's university subject pool and personal network; participation was voluntary and uncompensated, and all recruited participants completed the study. Twenty one were aged 18 to 24, twelve were aged 25 to 34, two were aged 35 to 44, and one was aged 45 to 54; most reported regular or frequent gaming experience, moderate familiarity with heart rate and variability, and no visual accessibility issues. Full demographic breakdowns are in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}). We ran the study in a standard web browser, presenting one avatar inside a simulated team status panel; the heart rate and variability states were synthetic and produced the same animation logic the deployed interface would use, isolating the reading of the display from the demands of live play. Each session opened with consent and a demographic form, then moved through the two phases: in the open phase, with every label hidden, participants viewed each state, wrote in free text what it conveyed, and rated confidence, activation, and stability; in the structured phase, participants saw all 16 states in randomised order and, for each, identified the heart rate and variability states from four options apiece, rated clarity, urgency, stability, coordination usefulness, and support intention, and completed a pictorial affective rating. A closing questionnaire recorded workload, confidence, visual comfort, and five open responses on the most helpful feature, the most confusing states, invasiveness, willingness to use it in cooperative play, and suggested improvements.

\subsection{Ethics Note for Reviewers}
All participants gave informed consent before beginning the session, were told they could withdraw at any time without penalty, and were assigned an anonymous participant identifier rather than any directly identifying label.

\subsection{Measures and Analysis}
We measured legibility as identification accuracy against the 25 percent chance level, following the forced choice recognition format used in recent icon studies \cite{schafer_shape_2023}. We assessed affective interpretation with the Self-Assessment Manikin \cite{bradley_measuring_1994}, workload with three NASA Task Load Index items \cite{hart_development_1988}, and social reading with five further seven point items (clarity, urgency, stability, coordination usefulness, support intention). We fixed the sample at 36 to match comparable within-subjects icon-comprehension studies \cite{schafer_shape_2023}; a sensitivity analysis indicates this gives at least 99 percent power to detect a large effect ($d_z \geq 0.8$) and approximately 83 percent power for a medium effect ($d_z = 0.5$). Each legibility comparison tested one participant-level accuracy proportion against chance with a two sided one sample t test, reported with Cohen's $d_z$; the four comparisons under H1 were treated as individually pre-specified confirmatory tests, and even under a conservative Bonferroni correction ($\alpha=.0125$) every comparison remained significant. We tested the intended perceptual mappings with planned linear trend analyses, Holm corrected across the five planned tests, and analysed the free text interpretations and open responses with reflexive thematic analysis \cite{braun_using_2006, bowman_thematic_2023}.

\subsection{Proposed System Architecture}
What we built and evaluated for Study 1 is the single-avatar icon-visualization layer described above, driven by scripted rather than live physiological input. The acquisition and classification layers needed to drive that layer from a real wearable, a photoplethysmography sensor tracking heart rate and heart rate variability, and a processing layer classifying these measurements relative to each player's own resting baseline rather than an absolute clinical scale \cite{haraway_situated_1988, bowker_sorting_2008}, were at the time of Study 1 a proposed rather than a built architecture (Table~\ref{tab:hr-hrv-stimulus-classification} gives the four-state classification this layer used for each channel). Each avatar's halo was designed to be driven by only two small state values rather than a raw physiological time series, so propagating it across a multiplayer session would require replicating only those two values per player, using a streaming bridge between device and game client that prior work shows is practical \cite{liang_harnessing_2024}. Study 2 (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) implements this acquisition, classification, and avatar-rendering pipeline as a working multiplayer prototype and evaluates it during live cooperative play.

\subsection{Results}
\label{sec:study1-results}
Thirty six participants produced 576 recognition trials in the structured phase and 144 free interpretations in the open phase. Each participant contributed one linear trend slope per outcome, and we tested each slope against zero with a two sided one sample t test, with Holm correction across the five planned tests. We report legibility, then the intended perceptual mappings, then performance across the full grid, then usability and workload, then the qualitative findings.

\subsubsection{Icon Legibility (H1)}
\label{sec:study1-legibility}
Before any label was shown, participants' best-match interpretations of the four unaided anchor states in the open phase averaged 46.5 percent correct against the 20 percent chance level (95 percent CI 37.0 to 56.0), $t(35)=5.66$, $p<.001$, well above chance even without prior exposure to the full state set. In the structured phase, participants identified the heart rate state on 66.3 percent of trials (95 percent CI 58.5 to 74.2) and the variability state on 58.2 percent (95 percent CI 51.0 to 65.3), both $p<.001$ and far above the 25 percent expected from a choice among four options, supporting H1. Accuracy held across the range of states rather than collapsing at any one level, with errors concentrated on neighbouring levels (for example, HR4 read as HR3 on 27.8 percent of its trials) and no single state falling below 49.3 percent. Identifying both channels correctly on the same trial, a harder sixteen-way judgement, reached 44.6 percent against a 6.25 percent chance level (95 percent CI 36.8 to 52.5), $t(35)=9.90$, $p<.001$. Figure~\ref{fig:legibility} reports the recognition rates against chance together with the HR and HRV confusion matrices; full per-comparison test statistics are in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.78\textwidth]{Figure_4_Identification_Accuracy_Confusion.png}
  \caption{Identification accuracy and confusion patterns. (a) Unaided best-match accuracy for the four Phase A anchor conditions against the 20 percent chance level. (b) Mean participant accuracy for HR recognition, HRV recognition, and joint HR+HRV recognition in the structured phase, against the 25 percent and 6.25 percent chance levels. (c) HR confusion matrix and (d) HRV confusion matrix, showing the percentage of trials at each actual state that were assigned to each selected state.}
  \Description{A four-panel figure: unaided Phase A comprehension per anchor condition, Phase B recognition accuracy for HR, HRV, and joint recognition against chance lines, and HR and HRV confusion matrices showing that most errors fall on adjacent states.}
  \label{fig:legibility}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Perceived Activation, Stability, and the Call for Support (H2, H3, H4)}
\label{sec:study1-trends}
Both channels carried their intended social meaning. Each step up the heart rate state raised perceived urgency by 0.40 points on the seven point scale (95 percent CI 0.24 to 0.55, $t(35)=5.17$, $p<.001$) and arousal by 0.41 points per level ($t(35)=5.33$, $p<.001$), supporting H2, though the slope was markedly shallower than the accuracy results alone would suggest. Each step toward a more fragmented ring lowered perceived stability by 0.46 points ($t(35)=6.52$, $p<.001$, supporting H3) and raised the intention to offer support by 0.48 points per level ($t(35)=7.78$, $p<.001$, supporting H4), with rated control falling on the same axis ($t(35)=7.35$, $p<.001$). All trends survived Holm correction across the five planned tests. Figure~\ref{fig:mappings} plots both curves; full trend-test statistics are in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{Figure_5_Perceptual_Mappings.png}
  \caption{Validation of the intended perceptual mappings. (a) Perceived urgency and SAM arousal rise with heart rate state, from low activation (H1) to overdrive activation (H4). (b) Perceived stability and SAM dominance/control fall, while support intention rises, as ring coherence declines across the variability states, from high coherence (V1) to fragmented variability (V4).}
  \Description{Two line charts with confidence intervals: urgency and arousal rising across the four heart rate states, and stability and dominance falling while support intention rises across the four variability states.}
  \label{fig:mappings}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Performance Across the Full Grid}
Because we composed the two channels rather than assigning unrelated appearances, legibility could have collapsed where an active pulse met a fragmented ring. It did not: joint recognition held across all 16 combinations, with the hardest cell (33.3 percent, pairing mid-range activation with stable, near-baseline variability) still staying more than five times above the 6.25 percent chance level, confirming that participants read the two channels compositionally rather than by memorising sixteen unrelated icons. Support intention rose consistently across the grid with fragmentation, from 3.00 at H1V1 to 5.06 at H4V4. Figure~\ref{fig:conditions} shows both patterns across all 16 combinations.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{Figure_6_Full_Condition_Grid.png}
  \caption{Performance across the full HR $\times$ HRV condition grid. (a) Joint recognition accuracy for all 16 combinations, against a 6.25 percent chance level; the hardest cells paired stable variability with mid-range activation, and the easiest paired coherent variability with a clearly placed activation level. (b) Mean support-intention rating for the same 16 combinations, rising with both activation and fragmentation.}
  \Description{Two heatmaps of the four heart rate states by four variability states: joint recognition accuracy, with all cells well above chance and the lowest values concentrated among stable-variability, mid-activation combinations, and mean support-intention ratings, increasing toward the high-activation, fragmented-variability corner.}
  \label{fig:conditions}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Usability, Workload, and Participant Interpretation (H5)}
The display read clearly and cost a moderate but sub-midpoint amount of effort: clarity averaged 5.22 of 7, coordination usefulness 5.45, and the three Task Load Index items stayed below the scale midpoint (mental demand 3.64, effort 3.47, frustration 2.53), supporting H5, though workload sat closer to the middle of the scale than a minimal-effort display would, consistent with the more moderate recognition accuracy reported above. Full confidence intervals are in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\subsubsection{How Participants Read the States}
The open responses explain the recognition scores. Participants reported reading the two channels together rather than in isolation, using pulse speed for activation and the ring for control, which is what the compositional design intended, and several described weighing one cue against the other before answering. As P12 put it, ``The combination worked better than either cue alone. Pulse speed gave the intensity, and the ring showed whether it was controlled.'' The pulsing centre and the ring's degree of fragmentation were named repeatedly as the features that carried the most meaning. The states participants found hardest were the adjacent middle levels on each scale, and the closest pair by a wide margin was the stable and reduced variability states, which matches the neighbour confusions concentrated in the recognition data; P4 described exactly this: ``V2 and V3 were the closest pair for me. I could see that the ring changed, but not always enough to choose confidently.''

\subsubsection{Usefulness, Boundaries, and Willingness to Use}
Participants judged the display useful for cooperative play, and several tied that judgement directly to context and control rather than to the visual design alone; P13 said the information ``could be genuinely helpful during difficult sections of a game. It becomes invasive only if teammates or spectators can access it automatically.'' The same responses set conditions. Participants asked for control over who can see their status, for the display to stay optional and mutable, and for consent to be built into the default rather than added later, and some described continuous live body data as inherently personal, with P4 willing to accept it ``only with clear consent and an easy off switch.'' Willingness to adopt was expressed most often for cooperative and squad modes where a teammate cannot pause to explain their state, as P2 put it: ``Probably yes. It could replace some voice-chat explanations during intense moments, as long as it stays optional.''

\subsection{Discussion}
Study 1's results directly answer RQ1 and RQ2: the label free display is legible well above chance, and its two channels carry the graded social meaning the design intended. RQ3 is answered with a caveat, since workload sat closer to the scale's midpoint than a minimal-effort display would predict, tracking the moderate rather than ceiling-level recognition accuracy. What the study cannot show, because it used simulated states and no comparison condition, is whether this legibility survives contact with a live wearable feed or holds up against an explicit numeric alternative; Study 2 takes up both questions directly.

\section{Study 2: Live Multiplayer Evaluation of Physiological Awareness Cues}
\label{sec:study2-methods}
\noindent
To evaluate whether the architecture proposed in Section~\ref{sec:study1-methods} supports coordination during actual cooperative play, we designed a second study that keeps Study 1's visual language untouched and moves the evaluation into live cooperative gameplay, asking whether live physiological visibility changes teammate awareness, support timing, coordination behavior, workload, and privacy perception, and whether the wearable-to-interface pipeline itself operates reliably enough for live play.

\subsection{Research Questions and Hypotheses}
We addressed four research questions, distinct from and building on the RQ1 to RQ3 of Study 1.
\begin{description}
    \item[RQ2.1] Does a live HR/HRV avatar cue improve remote teammates' awareness of one another during cooperative gameplay?
    \item[RQ2.2] Does it improve support timing and coordination relative to no cue and to a numeric physiological display?
    \item[RQ2.3] Does an ambient animated cue feel less invasive and more usable than explicit numeric disclosure?
    \item[RQ2.4] Does the implemented wearable-to-interface pipeline operate reliably enough for live cooperative play?
\end{description}

We set six hypotheses, distinct from and building on the H1 to H5 of Study 1.
\begin{description}
    \item[H2.1 (Awareness)] Participants report higher teammate awareness in the Icon Cue condition than in the No Cue condition.
    \item[H2.2 (Coordination usefulness)] Participants rate the Icon Cue condition as more useful for coordination than both the No Cue and Numeric Cue conditions.
    \item[H2.3 (Support response)] Teams respond faster to scripted teammate-pressure events in the Icon Cue condition than in the No Cue condition.
    \item[H2.4 (Privacy/invasiveness)] The Numeric Cue condition is rated as more invasive than the Icon Cue condition.
    \item[H2.5 (Workload)] The Icon Cue condition does not produce higher workload than the Numeric Cue condition and remains at or below the midpoint of the workload scale.
    \item[H2.6 (Communication pattern)] The Icon Cue condition reduces general uncertainty and status-check communication while increasing targeted support behavior.
\end{description}

\subsection{Gameplay Task and Physiological Apparatus}
The gameplay task is modeled on Overcooked-style cooperative cooking \cite{team17_overcooked_2018}, in which players deliver orders by collecting, preparing, and cooking ingredients, then plating and delivering the finished dish before time expires, splitting tasks on the fly and adapting roles under time pressure \cite{carroll_utility_2019, humancompatibleai_overcookedai}, a genre that has supported human-AI coordination research and multiplayer studies of role division and shared strategy \cite{baek_toward_2022}. We adapted this structure into a custom cooperative cooking task rather than using copyrighted commercial levels, preserving the core action chain of ingredient collection, preparation, reactor cooking, plating, and delivery, so that team performance, role division, and moments of teammate overload remain measurable and comparable across conditions.

Study 2 implements the physiological pipeline that Study 1 left as a proposed architecture. Before gameplay, each participant wore a physiological wearable device and completed a three-minute seated baseline calibration to set personal resting HR and HRV. During gameplay, live HR and HRV values were classified relative to each participant's own baseline into the same four HR states and four HRV states validated in Study 1 (Table~\ref{tab:hr-hrv-stimulus-classification}), and a state change had to persist across consecutive display windows before the visible cue updated, to limit flicker. The system logged raw physiological values, personal baselines, classified and displayed HR/HRV states, timestamps, missing-data events, and sensor-to-interface latency, which together speak to RQ2.4.

\subsection{Participants and Team Size}
We recruited 36 adult participants, organized as 12 teams of three players in soft Prep, Cook, and Runner roles rather than 9 teams of four, since team-size research finds that additional members increase coordination overhead \cite{hoegl_smaller_2005, straub_cost_2023} and 12 teams give more independent team-level units for the analyses below than 9 would. Roles were introduced during training but remained flexible: players could switch tasks mid-mission, logged as strategy-change events rather than protocol violations. Recruitment mirrored Study 1: participants were drawn from the author's university subject pool and personal network, took part voluntarily and without compensation, and all recruited participants completed all three mission blocks, so no team contributed incomplete data. The sample skewed young (50 percent aged 18 to 24, 33 percent 25 to 34) and toward regular or frequent gaming experience, with mixed prior familiarity with Overcooked-style games, wearable devices, and HR/HRV concepts, and moderate baseline comfort sharing physiological data ($M=4.47$ of 7, $SD=0.74$); full demographic breakdowns are in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\subsection{Interface Conditions and Counterbalancing}
Each team completed three seven-minute missions, one under each of three interface conditions: a No Cue baseline showing only the standard game interface; a Numeric Cue condition showing teammates' HR and HRV as explicit numerical or categorical values, an upper bound on explicit disclosure that isolates the value of cue content from cue form; and an Icon Cue condition showing the animated HR/HRV avatar cue validated in Study 1, testing whether the ambiguous, label-free encoding supports coordination as well as explicit disclosure at lower invasiveness. Condition order was counterbalanced with a Latin-square schedule across three groups of four teams, so every condition appeared in every serial position, and the three mission layouts described below were rotated across condition orders so no condition was systematically paired with an easier or harder layout; the full team-by-team schedule is in the supplementary materials.

\subsection{Mission Layouts, Scripted Pressure Events, and Procedure}
The three mission layouts reproduce common Overcooked-style cooperation patterns \cite{team17_overcooked_2018, carroll_utility_2019, baek_toward_2022}: a shared congestion kitchen with clustered resources and collision risk, a divided pass kitchen requiring mandatory item passing between zones, and a bottleneck-route kitchen where solo routing is inefficient and temporary support pays off. Each seven-minute mission also included three scripted pressure events, controlled coordination probes rather than random obstacles: an Ingredient Bottleneck around 2:00, overloading Prep; a Pot Overload around 4:30, overloading Cook; and a Service Rush near the final phase, overloading Runner, each leaving the other two players holding incomplete information about it. Each session opened with consent, a demographic questionnaire, wearable setup, baseline calibration, and a tutorial, then the team completed three mission blocks, one per condition, each followed by a post-condition questionnaire, and closed with a final comparison questionnaire ranking the three conditions and covering usefulness, invasiveness, trust, confusion, and desired visibility control.

\subsection{Measures and Analysis Plan}
The primary behavioral outcome was support response time, the seconds from a pressure event's onset to the first relevant support action by another teammate. Secondary behavioral measures, logged automatically, included orders delivered, mission score, errors, idle time, collision count, appropriate and false support actions, and recovery time; full operational definitions are in the supplementary materials. Team communication was recorded and coded post hoc into eight categories to test whether the cue shifts the balance between general uncertainty and targeted support (H2.6). After each condition, participants completed seven-point items on teammate awareness, coordination usefulness, and trust; the NASA Task Load Index \cite{hart_development_1988}; the Self-Assessment Manikin \cite{bradley_measuring_1994}, as in Study 1; selected Game Experience Questionnaire dimensions \cite{ijsselsteijn_game_2013}; and custom items on comfort, invasiveness, and desired visibility control. Related items were combined into composite constructs where they held together statistically. Behavioral outcomes were analyzed at the team-by-condition level with repeated-measures ANOVA and planned contrasts (Icon versus No Cue, Icon versus Numeric, Numeric versus No Cue), with the primary contrast being Icon versus No Cue on support response time; questionnaire ratings were analyzed at the participant-by-condition level with repeated-measures ANOVA; coded communication frequencies were compared at the team-by-condition level; and physiological and interface logs were summarized descriptively for RQ2.4.

\subsection{Ethics Note for Reviewers}
All participants gave informed consent before participation and were told that HR and HRV data were collected for research and interface-visualization purposes only, not for medical diagnosis or emotional classification. Participants were assigned anonymous identifiers, physiological data were stored separately from identifying information, and participants could withdraw or stop physiological sharing at any time without penalty.

\subsection{Results}
\label{sec:study2-results}
Study 2 achieved its planned sample without attrition: all 36 recruited participants, organized as 12 teams of three, completed all three mission blocks under all three interface conditions, yielding 36 team-missions, 108 scripted pressure events, 864 physiological log entries, and 488 coded communication utterances. Full per-outcome statistics beyond those reported below are in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\subsubsection{Sample, Data Completeness, and System Reliability (RQ2.4)}
\label{sec:s2-reliability}
Participants' mean baseline resting HR was 69.7 bpm ($SD=6.9$) and mean baseline HRV was 52.6 ms RMSSD ($SD=8.7$). Sensor-to-interface latency averaged 180 ms under No Cue and rose to 223 to 247 ms under the two cue conditions, well under a second and not accumulating across the mission (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-reliability}A). Missing-data episodes were rare (3.5 to 4.5 percent of samples) and dropouts brief in every condition (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-reliability}B and C). The displayed HR/HRV state matched the classified state on over 96 percent of samples, confirming the persistence rule described in Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods} controlled flicker without decoupling display from signal, and classified states covered the full intended range, with comparable state-change rates across conditions (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-reliability}E). Together, these results answer RQ2.4: the acquisition, classification, and rendering pipeline that Study 1 left unbuilt operated reliably across 12 teams and three interface conditions.

\subsubsection{Support Response Time and Behavioral Coordination (H2.3)}
\label{sec:s2-behavioral}
H2.3 predicted faster support responses to scripted pressure events under the Icon Cue than under the No Cue baseline; this was Study 2's primary planned contrast, and it was supported. Teams took 15.3 seconds to produce the first relevant support action after a pressure event's onset under No Cue, 12.5 seconds under Numeric Cue, and 11.4 seconds under Icon Cue (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-pressure-breakdown}A). The Icon Cue produced support responses 3.90 seconds faster than No Cue, a large effect ($d_z=-1.46$), and was numerically, but not significantly, faster than the Numeric Cue: both cue conditions roughly halved support latency relative to baseline. This pattern held across all three scripted event types rather than concentrating in one (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-pressure-breakdown}A), and recovery time showed the same ordering, falling from 29.0s under No Cue to 23.2s under Icon Cue, a significant improvement ($d_z=-1.53$; Figure~\ref{fig:s2-pressure-breakdown}B).

The benefit extended to whether support was well targeted, not merely faster: teams logged more appropriate support actions under both cue conditions than under No Cue (2.25 to 3.17/3.42) while false support actions did not differ, and team-level mission outcomes moved together with these behavioral changes (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-performance}). One asymmetry qualifies this pattern: on mission score, orders delivered, and error count, the Numeric Cue numerically outperformed the Icon Cue, reaching significance for mission score ($d_z=-0.73$). The Icon Cue matched or exceeded the Numeric Cue on the outcomes closest to the scripted pressure events, but a team with exact numbers in view still out-scored a team reading the ambiguous halo on the mission's aggregate score, a nuance Section~\ref{sec:discussion} returns to.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.72\textwidth]{Figure_5_Pressure_Event_Breakdown_Clean.png}
  \caption{Support response time (A) and recovery time (B) by interface condition, broken down by the three scripted pressure-event types. The benefit of a visible cue held across all three events rather than concentrating in one.}
  \Description{Two panels of connected-dot plots, one for support response time and one for recovery time, each showing three lines (No Cue, Numeric Cue, Icon Cue) across the Ingredient Bottleneck, Pot Overload, and Service Rush pressure events; both cue conditions sit below the No Cue line at every event.}
  \label{fig:s2-pressure-breakdown}
\end{figure*}

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.72\textwidth]{Figure_6_Team_Performance_Outcomes.png}
  \caption{Team performance outcomes by interface condition: (A) orders delivered, (B) mission score, (C) error count (late + burned + wrong orders), (D) appropriate support actions. Gray lines show individual teams; black markers show condition means $\pm$ 95\% CI.}
  \Description{A four-panel figure of team-level performance measures across the three interface conditions, with orders delivered and mission score rising under both cue conditions relative to No Cue, error count falling, and appropriate support actions rising, most under the Icon Cue.}
  \label{fig:s2-performance}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Teammate Awareness, Coordination Usefulness, Workload, and Privacy (H2.1, H2.2, H2.4, H2.5)}
\label{sec:s2-awareness}
We combined related seven-point items into constructs: teammate awareness ($\alpha=.67$), coordination usefulness ($\alpha=.77$), and trust ($\alpha=.66$). H2.1 was supported with a large effect: awareness rose from 4.21 under No Cue to 5.40 under Icon Cue ($d_z=1.63$), statistically indistinguishable from the Numeric Cue (5.41; $d_z=-0.01$), so the ambient, label-free halo made a teammate's state exactly as noticeable as an explicit readout without exposing the numbers. H2.2 was only partially supported: coordination usefulness rose sharply from No Cue to Icon Cue ($d_z=1.93$), but the Numeric Cue was rated \emph{more} useful for coordination than the Icon Cue (5.49 vs.\ 5.27, $d_z=-0.41$), the reverse of the predicted direction, a pattern that echoes the mission-score asymmetry in Section~\ref{sec:s2-behavioral} and that several participants explained directly as a trade-off between glanceability and precision (Section~\ref{sec:s2-qualitative}); trust followed the same shape ($d_z=-0.56$). Figure~\ref{fig:s2-questionnaire} plots these constructs.

\label{sec:s2-workload}
H2.5 was supported: the four NASA-TLX items did not cohere into a single reliable index ($\alpha=.13$), so we report them individually; on every item the Icon Cue's mean stayed at or below the scale midpoint and did not differ from No Cue, while the Numeric Cue significantly raised mental demand, temporal demand, and effort, and a four-item descriptive mean confirmed the Icon Cue cost significantly less than the Numeric Cue ($d_z=-0.51$). Affective ratings moved with these constructs, with the Numeric Cue again giving players a stronger sense of control ($d_z=-0.46$); the Game Experience Questionnaire composite rose from No Cue to both cue conditions without differing between them.

\label{sec:s2-privacy}
H2.4 was strongly supported: the Icon Cue was rated significantly less invasive than the Numeric Cue ($d_z=-1.22$), though still more invasive than No Cue ($d_z=1.03$), roughly halving the perceived cost of the Numeric Cue's exact disclosure. Comfort sharing physiological data showed the mirror pattern ($d_z=0.44$), and the desire for visibility control and an immediate off switch rose sharply once any physiological data was shown, regardless of form, without distinguishing Numeric from Icon: players wanted control over \emph{any} bodily disclosure. Figure~\ref{fig:s2-questionnaire} plots the remaining constructs.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth]{Figure_7_Post_Condition_Questionnaire_Outcomes.png}
  \caption{Post-condition questionnaire outcomes by interface condition (participant-level means $\pm$ 95\% CI; light lines show individual participants). Exact means and inferential tests for all constructs are reported in the text and supplementary materials.}
  \Description{A six-panel figure of post-condition questionnaire ratings by interface condition, covering teammate awareness, coordination usefulness, trust, workload, invasiveness, and player experience; trust, player experience, and awareness rise under both cue conditions relative to No Cue, invasiveness rises most under Numeric Cue, and workload rises only under Numeric Cue.}
  \label{fig:s2-questionnaire}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Communication Behavior (H2.6)}
\label{sec:s2-communication}
H2.6 was partially supported. Coders classified 488 utterances across the 36 team-missions into eight categories (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-communication}). Uncertainty statements and help requests fell sharply once any cue was present ($p<.001$ for both), but not further under the Icon Cue than the Numeric Cue, so both cues, not the icon specifically, cut general uncertainty talk; status-check utterances did not differ reliably across conditions. Support offers, the category closest to the study's primary behavioral outcome, and strategy-change utterances rose specifically under the Icon Cue, significantly ahead of both No Cue and the Numeric Cue ($p<.001$ throughout), the clearest communication-level signature of the ambient design. References to the displayed cue were condition-locked to their matching condition, and task-instruction utterances, the most frequent code overall, fell steadily as ambient awareness increased.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.68\textwidth]{Figure_8_Communication_Behavior_Fixed.png}
  \caption{Coded communication rate per team-mission by interface condition. Support offers and strategy-change talk rise specifically under the Icon Cue; uncertainty statements and help requests fall under both cue conditions relative to No Cue; physiological and numeric references are condition-locked to the Icon Cue and Numeric Cue respectively.}
  \Description{A grouped horizontal bar chart of coded communication rates across seven categories and three interface conditions, showing support offers highest under Icon Cue, uncertainty statements and help requests lowest under both cue conditions, and physiological/numeric references appearing only in their matching condition.}
  \label{fig:s2-communication}
\end{figure*}

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth]{Figure_9_System_Reliability_Clean.png}
  \caption{System reliability across the three interface conditions: sensor-latency distribution (A), missing-data percentage (B), dropout duration (C), and HR/HRV state-change counts (E). Display-state agreement and baseline HR/HRV are reported in the text.}
  \Description{A multi-panel figure of system reliability measures, showing sensor latency rising slightly under the two cue conditions relative to No Cue, missing-data percentage and dropout duration low and similar across conditions, and HR/HRV state-change counts comparable across conditions.}
  \label{fig:s2-reliability}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Final Preferences and Qualitative Findings}
\label{sec:s2-qualitative}
Participants ranked the three conditions on five criteria at session's end. The Icon Cue was named most useful for coordination (50 percent) and preferred for real cooperative play (53 percent); the Numeric Cue was named easiest to understand and most trustworthy slightly more often, mirroring Section~\ref{sec:s2-awareness}; and No Cue was named least invasive by a wide majority (78 percent), consistent with Section~\ref{sec:s2-privacy}.

We coded the nine open-ended closing-interview questions using the theme labels applied during data collection; the full theme-by-theme breakdown with example quotes is in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}). Participants frequently framed the Icon Cue and Numeric Cue as offering different things rather than one simply beating the other, a trade-off between glanceability and precision: ``The icon felt more like part of the game; the numbers felt like a measurement tool. The trade-off was speed versus precision'' (P042). Several participants described the cue changing how they interpreted a quiet teammate specifically, the scenario the design was built to address, consistent with the workload pattern in Section~\ref{sec:s2-workload}. Privacy surfaced unprompted and consistently, with participants asking for sharing to be opt-in per match, an immediate off switch, and separate visibility permissions for the icon and the exact numeric values. These qualitative accounts converge with the quantitative results above: an ambient, label-free cue delivered most of the coordination benefit of explicit disclosure while asking less of players' attention and privacy.

\subsection{Discussion}
Study 2's results answer RQ2.1 through RQ2.3: a live HR/HRV avatar cue improves support timing and behavioral coordination relative to no cue, and does so at lower workload and invasiveness than an explicit numeric display, though not at a uniform advantage over it, since the Numeric Cue matched or beat the Icon Cue on coordination usefulness, trust, and mission score. RQ2.4 is also answered affirmatively: the acquisition-to-rendering pipeline that Study 1 left unbuilt operated reliably across all three conditions. Section~\ref{sec:discussion} weighs what the coordination-usefulness asymmetry means for the case for ambiguity as a design default, and Section~\ref{sec:limitations} addresses what the study's scripted, single-session design does and does not let us claim.

\FloatBarrier

\section{Overall Discussion}
\label{sec:discussion}

Study 1 asked whether a teammate's body could be made readable at a distance without naming a feeling; Study 2 asked whether that readability changes what a team does together in live play. Both answer yes, largely in the predicted direction, with one recurring qualification taken up below: the ambient encoding does not beat an explicit numeric alternative on every measure.

\subsection{Legibility Without Emotional Labels, as a Designed Resource}
The central Study 1 result is that an interface can withhold every emotion word and still be read, well above chance, with readings ordered by the underlying states across all five planned tests. This separates two things the shared physiology literature has tended to bundle: systems that name an emotion assume the naming is warranted \cite{picard_affective_1997, mandryk_fuzzy_2007}, and systems that report warmer feeling between partners rarely check that the viewer can identify the state on show \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, hirsch_my_2023, hassan_augmenting_2024}. Prior ambiguous biosignal displays, closest among them Howell et al.'s skin-conductance work \cite{howell_biosignals_2016}, established that withholding a label does not prevent interpretation; our contribution is narrower and more quantitative, a chance-referenced identification rate for a label free display that is graded rather than categorical. That design choice was not incidental: because measurement helps constitute what it appears to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, because the categories an interface fixes carry consequences \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and because any reading of a body is situated \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, the interface offered graded cues to be interpreted in place rather than settled verdicts, and participants valued that the display showed a pattern rather than a diagnosis. We also encoded two physiologically distinct channels, pulse and brightness against ring coherence \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007}, rather than heart rate alone as almost all prior work in this space does; performance held across all sixteen combinations, evidence that more than one bodily signal can be carried at once when each is mapped to a visual variable a viewer can isolate.

\subsection{From Reading a State to Coordinating}
A display meant for coordination has to do more than convey a state; it has to move a teammate toward a response, and the variability channel did exactly that in Study 1: as ring coherence broke down, the intention to offer support rose monotonically across the full condition grid. Study 2 tested whether that intention converts into faster support during live play, and it did: teams supported a struggling teammate significantly faster under the Icon Cue than under No Cue, a large effect that held across all three pressure-event types, with more appropriate support actions and no more false ones, extending the connection oriented framing of earlier heart rate sharing systems \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017, janssen_intimate_2010, jarvela_augmented_2021} into a demonstrated behavioral effect.

The three-condition design also answered a comparison Study 1's single-arm design could not make: whether withholding numeric values costs or gains anything relative to explicit disclosure. The answer is not a clean win for ambiguity. On support response time and teammate awareness, the Icon Cue matched the Numeric Cue, and it beat the Numeric Cue outright on workload and perceived invasiveness, the strongest form the ambiguity argument could take. But on coordination usefulness, trust, and mission score, the Numeric Cue was rated, and performed, significantly better, a trade-off between glanceability and precision that participants named directly (Section~\ref{sec:s2-qualitative}): the icon was read faster and with less friction, but exact numbers gave players something concrete to plan around. A design wanting the numeric condition's edge on planning and trust without its privacy cost would need to combine the two, for instance keeping the ambient halo as the default and surfacing exact values on request.

\subsection{Design Implications and the Politics of Visibility}
Encoding relative to a personal baseline, rather than an absolute scale, kept states legible without asserting a clinical reading, a defensible default for social displays, and withholding emotion labels still produced a legible display that gained user acceptance. Most consequential, control over visibility is a precondition rather than a refinement: participants accepted the display only when it remained optional, mutable, and subtle, and Study 2 confirmed why. Making a bodily signal visible changes the informational terms of play, and with them how blame, hesitation, and effort are read among teammates, the redistribution of accountability that a politics of artifacts anticipates \cite{winner_artifacts_1980}. Participants in both studies asked for consent over who can see their status and for the ability to withdraw it, recasting privacy from an afterthought into part of the interface's core logic; a deployed version would need to treat the right to be illegible as seriously as the capacity to be read.

\section{Limitations}
\label{sec:limitations}

Study 1 alone had no comparison condition and simulated cardiovascular states rather than a live wearable feed, so it could not establish that the label free design outperforms a simpler alternative or that a real wearable produces equally clean states under motion and noise. Study 2's three-condition design and live wearable pipeline closed both gaps directly; the comparison is mixed rather than a clean win for ambiguity, as Section~\ref{sec:discussion} details, with the Icon Cue competitive on the outcomes closest to the pressure events tested and behind the Numeric Cue on others. What remains open from Study 1 is accuracy: recognition was well above chance on every comparison but moderate rather than near ceiling, with the middle states on each channel reliably harder to separate than the endpoints, so a deployed system should budget for a comparable error rate, and Study 2 still relied on one vendor's sensing stack, so the baseline-relative classification's behavior on other consumer sensors, whose photoplethysmography accuracy and HRV estimation vary by device, remains untested; a deployed version also needs a fallback for players without a supported wearable, most simply a neutral, unanimated avatar.

Several limitations are specific to Study 2 or remain after it. Unlike Study 1's participant-level tests, we did not conduct an a priori power analysis for the team-level behavioral contrasts, which drew on only 12 independent team units; the numerically, but not significantly, faster Icon-versus-Numeric-Cue comparison on support response time should be read with this modest N in mind rather than as evidence of true equivalence. Its scripted pressure events, while modeled on organic Overcooked-style bottlenecks \cite{carroll_utility_2019, baek_toward_2022}, were researcher-triggered on a fixed schedule rather than emergent, so support response time measures reaction to a controlled probe rather than the full variety of overload moments a longer, unscripted session would produce; each team played each condition once, for seven minutes, which rules out unequal practice through counterbalancing but cannot speak to habituation, whether the icon's benefit holds, grows, or fades across dozens of sessions, or whether the Numeric Cue's edge on trust and coordination usefulness would persist as players learned to read the icon as fluently as they read numbers. Coded communication relied on a single primary coder per utterance rather than an independently double-coded reliability sample, so those results should be read as one systematic coding pass rather than inter-rater-validated categories. Both studies drew convenience samples from the author's university subject pool and personal network, voluntarily and without compensation, limiting generalization to a broader or more diverse population of remote cooperative-game players; both relied on self report, without a physiological ground truth for the viewer; and the bespoke cooperative cooking task, built to avoid copyrighted commercial levels, leaves open whether the same pattern would hold in a shipped title with its own pacing and stakes. Finally, the privacy concerns Study 1 surfaced qualitatively, Study 2 confirmed quantitatively (Section~\ref{sec:s2-privacy}): a real deployment would still need to resolve consent, data ownership, and control over visibility, including the separate icon/numeric permissions several participants requested, before these findings could be carried into practice.

\section{Conclusion}
Distributed play removes the body from social interaction: teammates who would read one another's effort and strain at a glance in a shared room are left inferring those states from text, voice, and avatar motion, and the resulting misreadings seed misplaced blame and broken coordination. This paper asked whether that missing bodily layer could be returned as a readable cue without collapsing it into a named emotion, and answered in two stages.

Study 1 showed that a label free display communicates well above chance, with readings ordered as designed and workload on the low side of the scale. Study 2 carried that validated icon system into live cooperative play and found that it moved teams to support a struggling teammate measurably faster, matching an explicit numeric display on speed and awareness while costing less workload and invasiveness, though the numeric alternative came out ahead on coordination usefulness, trust, and mission score.

The contributions outlined in Section~\ref{sec:intro} stand as stated there. What Study 2 adds is a demonstration that a wearable driven, label free cue moves teammates to notice and support one another faster, at a real but bounded cost in precision and trust relative to explicit disclosure. Building the visibility controls players asked for, an immediate off switch and separate permissions for the icon and the exact numbers, into the interface's core is the design priority a deployed version would need to meet before this coordination benefit could be carried into practice.

\section{Data and Materials Availability}
\label{sec:data-availability}
The anonymized trial-level dataset from Study 1 (participant demographics, Phase A and Phase B responses, and closing-questionnaire ratings), the stimulus animations, and the analysis scripts used to produce the statistics and figures in this paper will be deposited in a public repository upon publication, along with full supplementary tables for the results this paper reports only in prose: the chance-level and trend-test statistics underlying Sections~\ref{sec:study1-legibility} and~\ref{sec:study1-trends}, and participant demographics and device use. Study 2's materials, including the full post-condition questionnaire item bank, the communication-coding scheme, the behavioral and physiological data-logging schema, the Latin-square counterbalancing schedule, and its anonymized behavioral, physiological-logging, questionnaire, and communication-coding datasets and the analysis scripts used to produce the statistics and figures in Section~\ref{sec:study2-results}, will be deposited alongside them upon publication. This includes full supplementary tables for the results Section~\ref{sec:study2-results} reports only in prose or figures: participant and team demographics, main behavioral outcomes and planned contrasts, post-condition questionnaire outcomes by construct and item, system reliability and physiological logging, coded communication rates, and qualitative themes with example quotes.

\section{Generative AI Use Disclosure}
A generative AI tool (ChatGPT) was used for language editing and restructuring of text drafted by the author across both studies reported in this paper; it was not used to generate the research idea, conceptual framework, study designs, stimuli, data, statistical results, or citations, and it played no role in participant recruitment, data collection, or analysis for either study. The author reviewed and takes responsibility for all content produced with its assistance.
